storm.summary_layperson: Radar is clear this evening — the slight-chance storm window is passing with no activity, and if a stray shower did develop, the cool Long Island Sound water would likely weaken it before it reaches the cove.
storm.local_effect_blurb: Sound water at 70°F — about 13°F cooler than the air — keeps a stable marine layer that tends to weaken incoming showers before they reach the shore.
storm.detail_html:
Radar at KOKX is clear — no storm cells anywhere in the monitored area as of the 5:22 PM scan. The NWS forecast carried a slight (20%) chance of an isolated shower or thunderstorm late this afternoon into early evening, and that window is now passing with nothing having developed over the region. The main sky story remains wildfire smoke aloft, with an Air Quality Alert in effect through midnight.

Atmospheric storm fuel is minimal: about 200 J/kg of CAPE now, fading to essentially zero after 8 PM. Winds aloft are strong (51 kt of deep-layer shear), which would organize storms efficiently if fuel were present — but at these instability levels there is nothing to organize.

The cove's usual summertime protection is active. Long Island Sound water is 70°F under low-80s air, a roughly 13°F gap that maintains a moderate stable marine layer over the water. Anything approaching from the west or southwest would have to cross that cool, stable air, which chokes a storm's low-level fuel supply and often weakens or splits it before the shoreline — the cove's risk runs lower than towns a few miles inland in this setup.

Most likely outcome for the evening: no precipitation through the racing hours and overnight. There are no upstream cells to track and instability decays with the daylight.

What would change the call: a cell firing over Westchester and riding the strong westerly steering flow could cover ground quickly with little warning — a rapidly building cumulus tower in the western sky around 6–7 PM would be the tell. The much better storm chance arrives Saturday, when widespread showers and thunderstorms are expected.

wind.regime_label: Moderate gradient wind — no sea breeze

hourly.shift_note: backed to W, light near sunset (8:25 PM)

wind.tactics_blurb: For the 7 PM race start: about 6 kt WNW at the gun, fading to 4 kt and swinging left toward due west during the first hour — favor the left side of the beat to ride the persistent left shift, keep clear air, and expect the last legs to be a light-air drift.
wind.tactics_detail_html:
This is a moderate gradient-wind day — no sea breeze in play. The air–water temperature difference (about 13°F: mid-80s air over a 70°F Sound) would normally support a thermal sea breeze, but the westerly background wind never let one establish — none of the Connecticut-coast or Long Island north-shore stations show a southerly component. The wind is the large-scale gradient, WNW across the whole network, and because this is a gradient-wind day rather than a sea-breeze day, the model direction is used directly (model and observations agree within 6°, so direction confidence is high).

Right now the Riverside YC station reads WNW (287°) at 11 kt, gusting 18 — lively, puffy late-afternoon conditions driven by daytime thermal mixing. That mixing is the breeze's life support, and it shuts down with the approaching sunset: expect roughly 9 kt at 6 PM, 6 kt at the 7 PM race start, and 4 kt by 8 PM, with the gust spread collapsing as the air steadies.

The direction tells the tactical story for the race window. The breeze holds WNW (near 300°) through the start, then swings left — counterclockwise — to due west (about 270°) between 7 and 8 PM as the daytime component fades out of the gradient. That is a roughly 30° persistent left shift during the first hour of racing: boats on the left side of the beat ride it as a growing lift, while boats that bang the right corner sail extra distance. Favor the left, and re-check laylines late in the beat — they will move.

In WNW flow the cove gets puffs channeled off the northwest shore with soft spots close under the Connecticut shoreline, so early in the race keep to pressure lanes mid-course-and-left rather than corner-hunting. After the shift completes, the westerly is light and fairly steady — position for clear air above all else.

Fleet impact: the first beat starts in workable 6-kt pressure but the race finishes in 4 kt or less — a light-air specialists' evening. Boat handling through the fade (smooth roll tacks, minimal rudder) will decide more than raw speed. Races that get off promptly at 7 PM enjoy the best of it; any second start will be a drifter.

data_caveats: Buoy WLIS (western Sound) wind observation was 73 minutes old at fetch time — used as background reference only.
